attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a one-hour history of sudden onset of clear fluid loss per vagina. She is currently 35 weeks pregnant in her third pregnancy with one previous birth. She can still feel fetal movements and denies any pain or contractions. She has no significant medical history apart from an allergy to benzylpenicillin. She does not take any regular medications. Her antenatal care has been uncomplicated to date.

On examination, she appears unwell with a pulse rate of 105/min, blood pressure 90/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 27/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 38.4°C. Abdominal examination reveals a singleton fetus with longitudinal lie and cephalic presentation. The fetal head is 5/5 palpable abdominally with no uterine tenderness. Speculum examination shows pooling of liquor in the posterior fornix with no evidence of cord prolapse and a closed cervix. The nitrazine test is positive. Blood tests reveal leucocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein. Endocervical swab is positive for chlamydia. Cardiotocography shows early decelerations with baseline fetal heart rate of 150/min and reduced variability.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most concerning finding that should prompt immediate delivery?
options: Maternal fever with elevated inflammatory markers and hypotension
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with preterm prelabour rupture of membranes at 35 weeks gestation. The combination of maternal fever, tachycardia, hypotension, and elevated inflammatory markers in this setting strongly suggests chorioamnionitis, which is a potentially life-threatening obstetric emergency requiring immediate intervention.

Maternal fever with elevated inflammatory markers and hypotension indicates maternal sepsis secondary to chorioamnionitis. This requires urgent multidisciplinary management including intravenous antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, and immediate delivery regardless of gestational age. Pregnant women can compensate well initially but may decompensate rapidly, leading to maternal and fetal compromise.

Positive endocervical swab for chlamydia requires antibiotic treatment for both the patient and her partner but is not an indication for immediate delivery. Chlamydia infection can be managed with appropriate antibiotics during pregnancy.

Early decelerations on cardiotocography can occur with preterm prelabour rupture of membranes due to oligohydramnios and cord compression. In the absence of other concerning features such as late decelerations or prolonged bradycardia, early decelerations alone do not mandate immediate delivery.

Gestational age of 35 weeks is not itself an indication for immediate delivery. In the absence of maternal or fetal compromise, expectant management would typically be considered at this gestation to allow further fetal maturation while monitoring for signs of infection or labour.

History of previous pregnancy loss is not relevant to the current clinical scenario and does not influence the decision for timing of delivery in preterm prelabour rupture of membranes.
learningPoints: Maternal fever, tachycardia, hypotension and elevated inflammatory markers in the setting of preterm prelabour rupture of membranes indicate chorioamnionitis requiring immediate delivery.
Chorioamnionitis is a potentially life-threatening obstetric emergency that requires multidisciplinary management including antibiotics, fluid resuscitation and expedited delivery.
Early decelerations on cardiotocography in the absence of other concerning features are not an indication for immediate delivery in preterm prelabour rupture of membranes.
references: Preterm prelabour rupture of the membranes. In: South Australian Perinatal Practice Guidelines [online]. September 2015. Accessed 2025 Jun 25. Available at: https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/public+content/sa+health+internet/clinical+resources/clinical+programs+and+practice+guidelines/perinatal+practice+guidelines/preterm+prelabour+rupture+of+the+membranes

Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG). Bacterial sepsis in pregnancy – 64a. 2012. RCOG Green top Guideline. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/en/guidelines-research-services/guidelines/gtg64a/ Accessed August 23, 2020

Sepsis in pregnancy. In: South Australian Perinatal Practice Guidelines [online]. June 2020. Accessed 2025 Jun 25. Available at: https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/public+content/sa+health+internet/clinical+resources/clinical+programs+and+practice+guidelines/perinatal+practice+guidelines/sepsis+in+pregnancy
Surviving Sepsis Campaign. In: Surviving Sepsis [online]. Accessed 2025 Jun 25. Available at: http://www.survivingsepsis.org

Bouvier D, Forest JC, Blanchon L, Bujold E, Pereira B, Bernard N, et al. Risk Factors and Outcomes of Preterm Premature Rupture of Membranes in a Cohort of 6968 Pregnant Women Prospectively Recruited. J Clin Med. 2019 Nov 15;8(11):1987. doi: 10.3390/jcm8111987. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31731659/
